sentences.text: Well, I was given a document by the American authorities at Buchenwald, and I had a choice to go anywhere, but I, I chose to go back to Munkacs, where I was born, raised, and where I left from, yes.
There, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from day one it was sheer devastation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't find my way.
I couldn't find myself.
I didn't know what this was all about.
An uncle of mine, my father's brother, met me by some coincidence as I was crossing the bridge, and I was about to go in to where I lived.
And he says, no, no, no.
You come.
You're coming to my place.
And I says, well, as if I didn't know, isn't anybody home there?
No, no, no.
I did run in.
I went in there, and there was some, some other people, a lady with a child actually was living there.
And I stood at the door, and she looked at me.
And she wanted to know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if there's anything she can do.
I says, no, no, no.
I'm sorry.
No, that's OK.
That's fine.
I, I, I used to live here, I said.
I just walked out of there.
She offered to even move out and, and, and give me the, the place.
It was not our own home.
It was like a, it was a four [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] living complex, you know.
We lived in one of them, and, and I just went, and of course with tears in my eyes I followed my uncle.
He carried my bundle.
And I went there, and I stayed with him for a very short period of time.
Because I'll tell you, it's not that-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you, you, you just couldn't live there.
It just didn't make sense to even go there, because it reminded you of what was.
And you look.
It just isn't here.
This is not the same place.
I look around, and I was used to seeing-- after all, Munkacs-- practically 50% of the population of Munkacs was Jewish.
I didn't see a Jewish child.
I couldn't see-- I couldn't-- there were about, I'd say, out of, out of 16,000 Jews that left Munkacs, I don't think we were 200 altogether.
So I decided to leave.
I left with a, a friend of mine.
We heard that, that Bucharest is intact, and that some restitution will also be meted out, given to us.
And we decided, two guys, you know, 18-year-olds, and went.
And we stayed there for about six weeks in Bucharest.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we had, you might say, a, a, a sort of warming type-- you know, we were well-received by the, by the Jewish population there and well-fed by Zionist organizations.
And-- but I just had to go back again to Munkacs, and I stayed there until January of 1946.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was difficult to get out of there because, you know, the borders were beginning to close on us, and the communists were not, I must say, there they were not very friendly to anyone for that matter, very brutal.
The Russians, the Red Army.

And so I, I sensed that this is not going to work either way, because every time you get out of the-- get out of your apartment, you know, you get picked up by a Russian patrol and taken to, to, to City Hall.
And they work you all day and give you a bowl of soup maybe and then send you back again.
And the next day the same thing happens over again.
So I decided to leave and I went, went to Slovakia, from Slovakia to Prague, and then from there the Zionist organization [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] got us moved to Germany.
And I ended up in a, in a displaced persons camp, Eschwege by castle.
And I was in constant search.
I never would give up.
Somehow, I don't know, I thought I had someone to look for, so I could never sit still.
And even though I was settled in the displaced persons camp, most of the time I traveled.
I went looking.
I went to another displaced persons camp, and I tried to find out at least from those who were with my father or were with my brother or knew what happened to my mother and my two sisters and so on.
And, and I did get quite a bit of details.

And that at least-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say that helped make a comeback, because I felt that I'm going to live here for one purpose, to know more and more and more and more, day by day, each day what did happen.
Why did it happen?
Why did it have to happen?
And then perhaps how is it possible to prevent it from happening again?
And this is what kept you going.
And that's what led me even back to Auschwitz in  '67 to revisit Auschwitz.
And since then, I lost track already how many times I went back.
Because that's the main purpose, in fact, from what I understand, knowing my colleagues who lived through that.
They are anxious to know why did it have to happen?
When did you come to the United States?
I arrived August the 31st, 1946.
Yeah.
All alone, oh yes.
Alone.
I was-- well, I arrived alone.

Of course, an uncle of mine, my mother's brother, who came to this country before I was even born, found my name in one of the Jewish organizations, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- I think it was the United-- one of the Jewish organizations.
And there was a roster there of Jews liberated in different camps.
And since I was liberated in Buchenwald, he says, Mermelstein, that's my sister's son.
I'm going to get him out.
Well, he went, but he couldn't really bring that about on his own.
The affidavit was supplied by a Jewish organization, yeah, by the joint distribution committee.
And that's how I arrived.
When I arrived, he was waiting for me at the boat, yeah, he and his wife, his son, his brother-in-law, and whatever else.
And then, and then there was the question of where would I go?
There were also-- because the Red Cross, because if you are under 21, they had responsibility, you know, to see that you are well-kept.
Do you remember what ship you came on?
Yeah, the SS it was called.
SS P-E-R-C-H.
It was a troop carrier, soldiers coming back from Europe.
And so he took me, and he said that you'll live with me.
And then I remember even the scene.
I'll never forget that, and that really frightened me, when he-- as we embraced, he said this is a beautiful country.
This is a rich country.
You're going to have a good life and forget what the dirty Nazis did to you.
And that kind of scared me.
And as a result of that, of course, I began jotting down from memory, in Hungarian-- that was my best language in 1946-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] jot down exactly what I experienced.
And I put into a book form.
Well, what I actually did was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go to school, you know.
I went to evening school and at the same time worked for the same uncle of mine.
And then I got so by 1947, '48, I was so advanced in my English and my studies that I registered for classes at, at City College of New York.
And education was really the, the biggest drive, the biggest thing.
I wanted to know, know, knowledge.
I was hungry for knowledge.
And that was my main thing at that time.
Of course, I went to school to City College and-- but then, part-time.
I also worked.
Then the Korean War broke out, of course, and when the Korean broke out-- war broke out, I was drafted in the American Army.
And it isn't to say that I had no choice, but once, once you're drafted, you have to submit yourself to the draft, which I did.
I gave them all the details, all the information, including when they asked you, do you have any visible markings on you?
Visible marking?
Yes, I have a tattoo.
And that was put down on my forms there.
So they knew that I was in a-- you know, in Auschwitz, but that did not prevent them from, from drafting me and, and processing me, with one exception.
When it came to being sworn in, that was the time the swearing-in officer did say that is there anyone here who is not a citizen of the United States?
Because before you're sworn in, you have a right not to serve or not to bear arms to defend the United States because you're not a citizen.
And I can tell you, I was among maybe a dozen other survivors of death camps.
Not one of them, not one of them said, no, I will not bear arms.
No, I will not defend the, the, you know, the United States.
We all did.
And we served.
I did not go to Korea.
Fortunately, they did not take me to Korea, but I did advance my studies at school.
In January of 1951, I became a, a regimental reporter, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and, and soon after that, when the regiment went back to Fort Meade, Maryland, I was selected for military intelligence.
And again, schooling.
This was perfect for me, you know.
I had good schooling, even in the military.

I also went to preliminary-- well, leadership school, it was called, which was a preliminary officers candidate school, but I did not go into officers candidate schooling, because there was no more time left.
My, my two years expired, and that was it.
I went back, back to New York, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, I was looking for a job.
I began to work, but I did not give up studying.
I went back to school.
And then in 1955 or '56 I got a job at the United, United Nations.
I worked as a temporary, you know, employee, but I loved it.
I mean, you know, I could have worked for the United Nations maybe forever [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for nothing.
An uncle of mine, in 1957, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who wasn't, wasn't married-- he was 48 years old, lived in Connecticut.
And he got a massive heart attack.
And he asked me to come to assist him, to help him.
He had a big business there and what have you.
But he died very shortly after.
He died, and he left a sizable estate.

And so between myself-- because I took my mother's share-- the uncle that met me at the, at the boat, and another uncle that I had that I didn't know who was living in South America, the three of us inherited his estate.
And from then on, I decided that New York is not a place where you can make money.
You can spend a lot of money there.
And I heard of a lot of survivors being on, on the West Coast.
And I decided to come out to the West Coast.
And I was very well received here.
I didn't do anything for a whole year.
I did have some money, and I didn't want to sponge it.
I didn't want to get rid of it.
I held onto the few dollars that I still held, had left, because I was married, married, and came--
Right then, in, you know--
Yes, I got married in New York.
And my wife and I, since my wife is an American born and I wanted her to know what she's going to have to live with, so--
At a dance, Sunday.
She came with two other friends.
And I sat there alone.
And she kept eyeing me.
And she want to know is it all right if we sit down over here.
I said, sure, why not?
And then we talked, and we danced.

And I'm told now, in fact-- we just came back from a wedding in North Carolina-- that her roommate-- because her roommate's daughter was getting married in North Carolina-- she said that I at that time already revealed to them who I was.
Meaning that they didn't understand the Holocaust.
We didn't talk about it in, in terms of the Holocaust, but I just explained to them a little.
You know, they saw the tattoo.
They were able to put two and two together.
And, and we had dinner after that.
And then we got to see each other more often, my wife and I.
And, and we got married, and then we decided to come west.
And this was definitely a better place to, to start a new life, even then.
And I began this business, this manufacturing business, pallets, wooden pallets, shipping containers, and so on.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then we had, of course, our son, the first one.
And then, and then I wanted a daughter.
And sure enough, we had a daughter.
And we have a daughter, we had another son and another son, so you got three sons and a daughter.
And we also have a grandson.
He's now six years old.
And that is a joy.
I-- it's, it's unbelievable that-- you have all that guilt-ridden feeling in you.
And I would say that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the building of a family is what makes life worthwhile.
It becomes a sort of a different life altogether.
You're no longer living for yourself.
And after all, bringing four children into this world and my grandchild [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is a big responsibility.
And it's a joy, too.
And perhaps without that, I don't think I could have, could have lasted as long as I did.
That I'm sure.
And it has nothing to do with the money aspect.
Money you can have, and money you can get rid of very, very quickly.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But nothing does for life as much as a, a family.
We did.
As a family, we did, but to the extent that, that it was Americanized, you know.
The Judaism that I was brought up with is, is, is not exactly the same.
While I have nothing against this modern Judaic way of living, I don't fit so well in it.
But as you heard, David, they identify with that, and-- but whatever you want to-- you talk about religion, they'll identify with religion.
But if you want, you want to know what they are, they're Americans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And rightly so.
I have never instructed them, so to speak, to be one or the other.
This has to flow, and you have to be comfortable with it.
And they had to put up with quite a bit just to come to the, to the point that they have in this the prejudicial and bigoted-- there's no question about it-- world that we're living in, sure.
Well, yes.
My wife had a lot to do with this in this respect.
She's a teacher, and she reads the newspapers from, from beginning to end.
And before she leaves, and if I'm still in the bedroom-- you know, in fact, a lot of times, she'll leave and I'm still half asleep.
She'll leave for me articles of interest.
And in 1980, it was just unbelievable, these, these, these denials.
All a sudden these, these, these, like, you know, Jews are angered because there is another view of some sort.
And then here comes this offer of a $50,000 reward to anyone who could prove that Jews were gassed in gas chambers at Auschwitz or that gassings took place, something to the effect.
And they didn't mean to offer it to specific individuals, to anyone.
I left it alone.
I wasn't going to touch it.
And my wife kept leaving those articles again, as if to say, aren't you going to do something about it?
Well, I wasn't about to do anything about it other than write a letter to the editor, which I did.
I wrote and about three, four newspapers published that letter to the editor in which I expose them to be what they are, a bunch of old Nazis, neo-Nazis or whatever, you know, and that's it.
Well, soon after that letter was published.
And it was also published in the international edition.
Suddenly I get a letter marked personal, right here to my office, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and it says, Dear Mr.
Mermelstein, from a letter to the editors, we are convinced that you can prove the Jews were gassed in gas chambers in Auschwitz.

Now-- and in this letter, they go back by stating that they had this offer to anyone, but, but they, they, they went ahead and, and, and dismissed it, and-- but now we want to renew that $50,000 reward for you, Mr.
Mermelstein, since you can, you can prove to us that Jews were gassed in gas chambers at Auschwitz.
And if you did not, we will expose you to the mass media.
That's the thing that didn't sit so well with me.
So as a result of that, I looked for some lawyers to help me do something about that letter, because it was offensive and whatever else.
I couldn't get the proper help, but I finally did have one lawyer who, who said, look, we can do it, but you better prepare yourself for, for a sort of a, a, you know, a-- oh, I don't know, whatever.
They're going to have a panel and, and whatever.
And in any case, that panel never came about.

They never even responded to our offer because this lawyer, Bill Cox, he, on my behalf, answered that letter and told them that they-- that we would do that, providing, you know, they will not renege their contract.
And we got into a contract with them.
They reneged the contract.
Took them to court.

And the very first thing we won was a judicial notice from a judge in a court of law, in Superior Court, that indeed Jews were gassed in gas chambers in Auschwitz and that the Holocaust is not subject to dispute, and it is simply a fact.
And that rang a bell.
That was, that was on October the 9th, 1991.
But of course, I didn't get the $50,000 then, because the judge did not want to get involved in anything other than just to take the judicial notice.
And then, Mr.
Mermelstein, if you want to get your $50,000, you will have to litigate further.
Well, we did.
We litigated until 1985, at which time we won.
I won a stipulated judgment for $150,000, not $50,000.
And I decided to reduce it to $90,000, providing, providing they pay a certain way.
You know, I don't want to get paid in 20 years.
I want to get paid a $50,000 check in a lump sum.
You offered me a $50,000 reward.
I want that $50,000 check.
And by the way, it's to be made out to Mel Mermelstein, personally, cashier's check.
Well, they first didn't agree.
Finally, they did agree.
And then I said there's one more thing I want.
I want a letter of apology from you, not only to Mel Mermelstein, the Auschwitz survivor, but all other Auschwitz survivors.
And we got that, too.
Oh, yes.
That rang a bell.
And I got paid for the first $50,000. 30 days later, I got another $20,000. 30 days after that, another $20,000 cashier's check, which came seven days late.
I wasn't so very happy about it.
But then there was a, well, a complaint filed.
And finally my lawyers came out with an additional $38.36 in late charges.
And they paid that, too.
So what else?
What else do you want from them?
Are you finished with that lawsuit then?
Oh, yeah.
Thank God it finally-- we finally finished, because there's a lot more to it.
Finally finished in 1991-- 1992.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We won, because they sued me for another $3 and 1/2-- they sued me for $3 and 1/2 million, and then I answered the complaint, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they dismissed the complaint.
And there were some, you know, back-and-forth disputes going.
And then the law firm that filed that lawsuit against me for $3 and 1/2 million on their behalf settled.
There was a good faith settlement for an additional $100,000.
From then on, I felt it's not worth going any further.
They threatened to sue me because I didn't want to go further, but it's all over now.
